title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a river clean-up group. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Ella, The Survey Mapper
Ella joined the river group because she enjoyed walking there and disliked seeing rubbish caught in the reeds. [47]Before anyone collected litter, she walked the banks with a map and marked places where volunteers could reach the water safely.[/47] Her day job in town planning helped her think about access points, paths and the need to keep emergency vehicles clear. [48]She created a simple numbering system for each section of river, so bags of rubbish could be reported without confusion.[/48] Ella found the first clean-up frustrating because rain changed the water level and made half her notes useless. However, she liked watching a messy stretch slowly become understandable and less intimidating for newcomers. She now says mapping is a promise to return, not just preparation. Her favourite discoveries are small: kingfisher flashes, children noticing snails, and residents finally learning the names of forgotten footbridges on routes they had used for years without looking.
texts.B:
### Jonah, The Wader Team Member
Jonah volunteered for the wader team after borrowing waterproof trousers from his uncle, who had once fished the same river. [49]He had expected mud, but he was shocked by the strength of the current even where the water looked shallow and harmless from the path.[/49] The safety briefing suddenly seemed much less boring and much more personal. His main task was freeing plastic bags from roots without damaging the plants holding the bank together. [50]One morning he found an old bicycle frame buried so deeply that three volunteers had to dig around it before it moved.[/50] The object became a symbol of how long neglect can remain hidden. [55]Jonah says the river taught him to work slowly, because a careless pull could cloud the water or harm wildlife.[/55] He still enjoys the heavy tiredness after a session, especially when his gloves smell of river mud and the bank behind him looks clearer.
texts.C:
### Nisha, The Schools Coordinator
Nisha persuaded local schools to join the project by designing short activities that matched science lessons and fitted into busy timetables. [51]She discovered that pupils were more interested in counting tiny pieces of plastic than in being told general textbook facts about pollution.[/51] Her experience as a classroom assistant helped her manage excitement near the water without sounding constantly alarmed or spoiling the sense of adventure. The most difficult part was convincing teachers that the visit would be useful even if the children collected very little rubbish. [52]After one session, a quiet pupil sent her a drawing showing where litter travelled after falling through a drain.[/52] Nisha realised the clean-up had worked because the child had understood a whole system, not because a bag was full. She now gives every class a magnifying glass, a clipboard and one rule: notice before you judge, because careful looking is where responsibility begins.
texts.D:
### Peter, The Volunteer Driver
Peter did not collect much rubbish himself; he drove the van that carried tools, bags, first-aid boxes and tired volunteers. [53]He learned that the route had to be planned around narrow bridges, locked gates and streets where the van could not turn round.[/53] His former job delivering furniture helped him load heavy sacks so sharp metal did not tear lighter bags. [54]The worst day was when a tyre punctured beside the river during a cold November session and the whole group waited while the council truck blocked the lane.[/54] Peter felt embarrassed, but the delay made everyone review the emergency plan properly over paper cups of tea. [56]He says practical logistics decide whether enthusiasm survives beyond the first weekend.[/56] He likes being the person who remembers spare gloves, drinking water and the list of phone numbers nobody thinks about until something goes wrong at the far end of the path.

questions.question: checked where people could safely reach the water before the cleaning began?
questions.explanation: Ella mapped safe access points along the riverbank before litter was collected.

questions.question: invented a numbered system to make reporting locations clearer?
questions.explanation: Ella numbered sections so rubbish bags could be reported without confusion.

questions.question: was surprised by a danger that was not obvious from the surface?
questions.explanation: Jonah was shocked by the strength of the current in shallow-looking water.

questions.question: helped remove a large object that was deeply buried?
questions.explanation: Jonah found a bicycle frame that required several volunteers to dig it out.

questions.question: found that children responded best to measuring a small concrete problem?
questions.explanation: Nisha saw pupils were interested in counting tiny plastic pieces.

questions.question: received evidence that a child had understood how pollution moves?
questions.explanation: A pupil sent Nisha a drawing showing litter travelling through a drain.

questions.question: had to plan transport around physical obstacles on the route?
questions.explanation: Peter planned the van route around bridges, locked gates and difficult streets.

questions.question: describes a vehicle problem that delayed the whole group?
questions.explanation: Peter had a puncture that made the group wait by the river.

questions.question: says careless action could disturb the water or damage animals and plants?
questions.explanation: Jonah says pulling carelessly could cloud the water or harm wildlife.

questions.question: believes planning and logistics determine whether volunteers keep coming?
questions.explanation: Peter says practical logistics decide whether enthusiasm survives.
